[[JA:能生白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11611996}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Nou Hakusan Shrine |Image = [[File:Hakusan-jinja Nou town Niigata Japan.jpg|270px]]
Shrine grounds |Location = {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}{{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208}} Ōaza Nou 7239 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|6|24.77|N|137|59|39.00|E|display=ti|region:JP-15|name=Nou Hakusan Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto
Izanagi-no-Mikoto
Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (candidate shrine) / Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded = Traditionally: 11th year of Emperor Sujin |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} style, thatched with cypress bark |Other names = Hakusan Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 24|Q2529|WD=Q2529}} |Rituals = Bugaku (court dance), lion dance (April 24) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Niigata|label=Nou
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nou Hakusan Shrine''' (のうはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Ōaza Nou, {{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208}}, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}. It is one of the candidate shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}) for the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Nunakawa Shrine.” Its official name is '''Hakusan Shrine'''According to the list of shrines on the Niigata Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines website.. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Nunakawahime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}}. Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto is a goddess who has long been worshipped throughout the region centered on Itoigawa City. She is known from the 『{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}}』 for her courtship and marriage with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, as well as the story of moving to Shinano together for the governance of the land. In ancient times, the {{ill|lt=Nishikubiki District|Q11629983|WD=Q11629983}} area of Niigata Prefecture was known as Nunakawa-no-shō. There are many legends and rituals related to Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto in the area surrounding Hakusan Shrine. In Ōaza Shimamichi within the city, slightly up the mountainside from the Shimamichi River (a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Nou River|Q134567545|WD=Q134567545}}, formerly said to be called Nunokawa), which flows from Mount Ōsawa, there is a large rock cave said to be the birthplace of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. Downstream in Omi is Omi Hakusan Shrine, whose enshrined deities are Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{ill|lt=Kukurihime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kikurihime-no-Mikoto}}. According to the origin legend of Omi Hakusan Shrine, the cave is also said to have been the residence when Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto married into the household of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto of Koshi. Mount Ōsawa is connected to Shindōzan (Shindō-no-mine), which is passed down in legend as the path Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto traveled to court Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. There are 1,088 stone steps leading to the summit. === History === According to a prewar “Prospectus of the Repair Donation Association,” the shrine is said to have been founded on the first Horse Day of the 11th month in the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Genshō|Q235343|WD=Q235343}}, the monk {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō Daishi}} deeply revered the shrine, installed Buddhist statues, and named it “Hakusan Gongen.” In the {{ill|lt=Kankō|Q2736722|WD=Q2736722}} era (early 11th century), a divided spirit was transferred from {{ill|lt=Shirayama Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|lt=Kaga Hakusan Shrine}} and enshrined together at the former shrine site. Nouzan Taiheiji Hōkōin flourished as a subsidiary hall of Hakusan Gongen, at one time possessing a complex of seven halls, 75 subsidiary shrines, and more than 50 temple lodgings. === Rituals === The “Nou Nunakawa Festival,” based on the long-told myths of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto and Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, features a bridal procession, ancient threshing rites, rice-polishing rituals, and mochi pounding. The Spring Grand Festival is held every year on April 24 and is locally known as the “Nou Festival.” Eleven pieces of bugaku and a lion dance are performed, and the final dance, “Ryōō no Mai,” is especially striking, with crimson costumes set against the evening sun of early spring. The bugaku was selected in {{ill|lt=1973|Q2477|WD=Q2477}} (Shōwa 48) as a National {{ill|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property Requiring Documentation|Q11642584|WD=Q11642584}} (Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property), and in 1980 (Shōwa 55) was designated as a National {{ill|lt=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Q6573893|WD=Q6573893}} under the name “Nou Bugaku” (see also “{{ill|lt=Itoigawa–Nou Bugaku|Q11604877|WD=Q11604877}}” and “{{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku|Q11579603|WD=Q11579603}}”). === Shrine buildings === The main hall is a {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=sangensha nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|lt=cypress bark roofing|Q199388|WD=Q199388}}, with a one-bay {{ill|lt=worship porch|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970}} at the front. Built in the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} ({{ill|lt=1515|Q6208|WD=Q6208}}), it retains characteristics of the Muromachi period. In 1958 (Shōwa 33), it was designated a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. The treasure hall houses Important Cultural Properties such as a wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, as well as ship ema and ship plaques designated as {{ill|lt=Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11644858|WD=Q11644858}}. === Shrine grounds === The shrine grove, Oyama (the sacred mountain), is said to mark the northernmost habitat of the {{ill|lt=Himeharu cicada|Q5976326|WD=Q5976326}}, and in {{ill|lt=1996|Q2070|WD=Q2070}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 8) it was selected as one of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan’s 100 Soundscapes|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === (The designation type and date are shown in parentheses after each item.) ;Important Cultural Properties (National) * Main hall, with 4 ridge tags attached (structure, May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33)) * Wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, 1 figure (sculpture, April 14, {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39)) : A single-block carving of cherry wood, made in the late Heian period. Around the topknot on the head are traces indicating that faces like those seen on Eleven-Headed Kannon statues were once attached. At present, the arms from the shoulders onward are missing, and traces remain showing that multiple arms were once joined. However, the joints are too small for a Thousand-Armed Kannon, and it is presumed that the statue was originally created as an Eleven-Headed Kannon with four arms (two on each side). Eleven-Headed Kannon statues are usually two-armed, but among those created as the honji-butsu of Hakusan, there are examples with four arms.Special Exhibition Catalog, “Niigata Buddhist Sculpture Exhibition: Special Exhibition Commemorating the Recovery from the Chuetsu Earthquake,” Niigata Prefectural Museum of Modern Art, 2006, p.130 ;Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Maritime faith materials of Nou Hakusan Shrine, 97 items (March 3, 1987 (Shōwa 62)) : Includes ship ema and ship plaques. The ships depicted on the ema are called Hagase-bune. ;Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Nou Bugaku (January 28, 1980 (Shōwa 55)) ;Natural Monuments (National) * Shrine grove of Nou Hakusan Shrine (December 21, 1937 (Shōwa 12)) * Habitat of the Nou Himeharu cicada (October 14, 1942 (Shōwa 17)) ;Niigata Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Bugaku masks, 4 pieces, with 1 additional piece (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze standing Eleven-Headed Kannon statue, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Wooden seated statue of Taichō Daishi, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze temple bell, 1 piece (craft, March 31, 1976 (Shōwa 51)) ;Itoigawa City Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Worship hall (structure, July 22, 1994 (Heisei 6)) * Group of Buddhist and Shinto statues, 43 figures (sculpture, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Ancient mirrors, 2 pieces (craft, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Complete Buddhist sutra written in gold on indigo paper, 1 scroll (calligraphy, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Vermilion-sealed documents of Tokugawa Iemitsu, 11 items (calligraphy, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) * Ridge tag, 1 piece (historical material, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) === Visitor information === ;Access * About a 20-minute walk northeast from {{ill|lt=Nou Station|Q5359845|WD=Q5359845}} ;Nearby * Benten Rock (a subsidiary shrine of Hakusan Shrine: Itsukushima Shrine) * Nou Beach === See also === * {{ill|lt=Hakusan faith|Q11579557|WD=Q11579557}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsu Shrine (Itoigawa City)|Q172253|WD=Q172253}} === References === {{Reflist}} === External links === * {{Official website}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nou Hakusan Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata {{ill|Nunakahano Shrine|lt=Nunakahano Shrine|WD=Q135040414}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|lt=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '新潟県糸魚川市大字能生7239', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:能生白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11611996}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Nou Hakusan Shrine |Image = [[File:Hakusan-jinja Nou town Niigata Japan.jpg|270px]]
Shrine grounds |Location = {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}{{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208}} Ōaza Nou 7239 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|6|24.77|N|137|59|39.00|E|display=ti|region:JP-15|name=Nou Hakusan Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto
Izanagi-no-Mikoto
Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (candidate shrine) / Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded = Traditionally: 11th year of Emperor Sujin |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} style, thatched with cypress bark |Other names = Hakusan Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 24|Q2529|WD=Q2529}} |Rituals = Bugaku (court dance), lion dance (April 24) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Niigata|label=Nou
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nou Hakusan Shrine''' (のうはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Ōaza Nou, {{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208}}, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}. It is one of the candidate shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}) for the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Nunakawa Shrine.” Its official name is '''Hakusan Shrine'''According to the list of shrines on the Niigata Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines website.. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Nunakawahime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}}. Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto is a goddess who has long been worshipped throughout the region centered on Itoigawa City. She is known from the 『{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}}』 for her courtship and marriage with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, as well as the story of moving to Shinano together for the governance of the land. In ancient times, the {{ill|lt=Nishikubiki District|Q11629983|WD=Q11629983}} area of Niigata Prefecture was known as Nunakawa-no-shō. There are many legends and rituals related to Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto in the area surrounding Hakusan Shrine. In Ōaza Shimamichi within the city, slightly up the mountainside from the Shimamichi River (a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Nou River|Q134567545|WD=Q134567545}}, formerly said to be called Nunokawa), which flows from Mount Ōsawa, there is a large rock cave said to be the birthplace of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. Downstream in Omi is Omi Hakusan Shrine, whose enshrined deities are Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{ill|lt=Kukurihime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kikurihime-no-Mikoto}}. According to the origin legend of Omi Hakusan Shrine, the cave is also said to have been the residence when Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto married into the household of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto of Koshi. Mount Ōsawa is connected to Shindōzan (Shindō-no-mine), which is passed down in legend as the path Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto traveled to court Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. There are 1,088 stone steps leading to the summit. === History === According to a prewar “Prospectus of the Repair Donation Association,” the shrine is said to have been founded on the first Horse Day of the 11th month in the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Genshō|Q235343|WD=Q235343}}, the monk {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō Daishi}} deeply revered the shrine, installed Buddhist statues, and named it “Hakusan Gongen.” In the {{ill|lt=Kankō|Q2736722|WD=Q2736722}} era (early 11th century), a divided spirit was transferred from {{ill|lt=Shirayama Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|lt=Kaga Hakusan Shrine}} and enshrined together at the former shrine site. Nouzan Taiheiji Hōkōin flourished as a subsidiary hall of Hakusan Gongen, at one time possessing a complex of seven halls, 75 subsidiary shrines, and more than 50 temple lodgings. === Rituals === The “Nou Nunakawa Festival,” based on the long-told myths of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto and Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, features a bridal procession, ancient threshing rites, rice-polishing rituals, and mochi pounding. The Spring Grand Festival is held every year on April 24 and is locally known as the “Nou Festival.” Eleven pieces of bugaku and a lion dance are performed, and the final dance, “Ryōō no Mai,” is especially striking, with crimson costumes set against the evening sun of early spring. The bugaku was selected in {{ill|lt=1973|Q2477|WD=Q2477}} (Shōwa 48) as a National {{ill|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property Requiring Documentation|Q11642584|WD=Q11642584}} (Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property), and in 1980 (Shōwa 55) was designated as a National {{ill|lt=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Q6573893|WD=Q6573893}} under the name “Nou Bugaku” (see also “{{ill|lt=Itoigawa–Nou Bugaku|Q11604877|WD=Q11604877}}” and “{{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku|Q11579603|WD=Q11579603}}”). === Shrine buildings === The main hall is a {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=sangensha nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|lt=cypress bark roofing|Q199388|WD=Q199388}}, with a one-bay {{ill|lt=worship porch|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970}} at the front. Built in the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} ({{ill|lt=1515|Q6208|WD=Q6208}}), it retains characteristics of the Muromachi period. In 1958 (Shōwa 33), it was designated a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. The treasure hall houses Important Cultural Properties such as a wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, as well as ship ema and ship plaques designated as {{ill|lt=Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11644858|WD=Q11644858}}. === Shrine grounds === The shrine grove, Oyama (the sacred mountain), is said to mark the northernmost habitat of the {{ill|lt=Himeharu cicada|Q5976326|WD=Q5976326}}, and in {{ill|lt=1996|Q2070|WD=Q2070}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 8) it was selected as one of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan’s 100 Soundscapes|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === (The designation type and date are shown in parentheses after each item.) ;Important Cultural Properties (National) * Main hall, with 4 ridge tags attached (structure, May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33)) * Wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, 1 figure (sculpture, April 14, {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39)) : A single-block carving of cherry wood, made in the late Heian period. Around the topknot on the head are traces indicating that faces like those seen on Eleven-Headed Kannon statues were once attached. At present, the arms from the shoulders onward are missing, and traces remain showing that multiple arms were once joined. However, the joints are too small for a Thousand-Armed Kannon, and it is presumed that the statue was originally created as an Eleven-Headed Kannon with four arms (two on each side). Eleven-Headed Kannon statues are usually two-armed, but among those created as the honji-butsu of Hakusan, there are examples with four arms.Special Exhibition Catalog, “Niigata Buddhist Sculpture Exhibition: Special Exhibition Commemorating the Recovery from the Chuetsu Earthquake,” Niigata Prefectural Museum of Modern Art, 2006, p.130 ;Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Maritime faith materials of Nou Hakusan Shrine, 97 items (March 3, 1987 (Shōwa 62)) : Includes ship ema and ship plaques. The ships depicted on the ema are called Hagase-bune. ;Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Nou Bugaku (January 28, 1980 (Shōwa 55)) ;Natural Monuments (National) * Shrine grove of Nou Hakusan Shrine (December 21, 1937 (Shōwa 12)) * Habitat of the Nou Himeharu cicada (October 14, 1942 (Shōwa 17)) ;Niigata Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Bugaku masks, 4 pieces, with 1 additional piece (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze standing Eleven-Headed Kannon statue, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Wooden seated statue of Taichō Daishi, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze temple bell, 1 piece (craft, March 31, 1976 (Shōwa 51)) ;Itoigawa City Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Worship hall (structure, July 22, 1994 (Heisei 6)) * Group of Buddhist and Shinto statues, 43 figures (sculpture, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Ancient mirrors, 2 pieces (craft, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Complete Buddhist sutra written in gold on indigo paper, 1 scroll (calligraphy, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Vermilion-sealed documents of Tokugawa Iemitsu, 11 items (calligraphy, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) * Ridge tag, 1 piece (historical material, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) === Visitor information === ;Access * About a 20-minute walk northeast from {{ill|lt=Nou Station|Q5359845|WD=Q5359845}} ;Nearby * Benten Rock (a subsidiary shrine of Hakusan Shrine: Itsukushima Shrine) * Nou Beach === See also === * {{ill|lt=Hakusan faith|Q11579557|WD=Q11579557}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsu Shrine (Itoigawa City)|Q172253|WD=Q172253}} === References === {{Reflist}} === External links === * {{Official website}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nou Hakusan Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata {{ill|Nunakahano Shrine|lt=Nunakahano Shrine|WD=Q135040414}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '新潟県糸魚川市大字能生7239', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:能生白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11611996}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Nou Hakusan Shrine |Image = [[File:Hakusan-jinja Nou town Niigata Japan.jpg|270px]]
Shrine grounds |Location = {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}{{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208}} Ōaza Nou 7239 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|6|24.77|N|137|59|39.00|E|display=ti|region:JP-15|name=Nou Hakusan Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto
Izanagi-no-Mikoto
Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (candidate shrine) / Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded = Traditionally: 11th year of Emperor Sujin |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} style, thatched with cypress bark |Other names = Hakusan Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 24|Q2529|WD=Q2529}} |Rituals = Bugaku (court dance), lion dance (April 24) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Niigata|label=Nou
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nou Hakusan Shrine''' (のうはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Ōaza Nou, {{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208}}, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}. It is one of the candidate shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}) for the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Nunakawa Shrine.” Its official name is '''Hakusan Shrine'''According to the list of shrines on the Niigata Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines website.. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Nunakawahime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}}. Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto is a goddess who has long been worshipped throughout the region centered on Itoigawa City. She is known from the 『{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}}』 for her courtship and marriage with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, as well as the story of moving to Shinano together for the governance of the land. In ancient times, the {{ill|lt=Nishikubiki District|Q11629983|WD=Q11629983}} area of Niigata Prefecture was known as Nunakawa-no-shō. There are many legends and rituals related to Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto in the area surrounding Hakusan Shrine. In Ōaza Shimamichi within the city, slightly up the mountainside from the Shimamichi River (a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Nou River|Q134567545|WD=Q134567545}}, formerly said to be called Nunokawa), which flows from Mount Ōsawa, there is a large rock cave said to be the birthplace of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. Downstream in Omi is Omi Hakusan Shrine, whose enshrined deities are Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{ill|lt=Kukurihime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kikurihime-no-Mikoto}}. According to the origin legend of Omi Hakusan Shrine, the cave is also said to have been the residence when Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto married into the household of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto of Koshi. Mount Ōsawa is connected to Shindōzan (Shindō-no-mine), which is passed down in legend as the path Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto traveled to court Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. There are 1,088 stone steps leading to the summit. === History === According to a prewar “Prospectus of the Repair Donation Association,” the shrine is said to have been founded on the first Horse Day of the 11th month in the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Genshō|Q235343|WD=Q235343}}, the monk {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō Daishi}} deeply revered the shrine, installed Buddhist statues, and named it “Hakusan Gongen.” In the {{ill|lt=Kankō|Q2736722|WD=Q2736722}} era (early 11th century), a divided spirit was transferred from {{ill|lt=Shirayama Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|lt=Kaga Hakusan Shrine}} and enshrined together at the former shrine site. Nouzan Taiheiji Hōkōin flourished as a subsidiary hall of Hakusan Gongen, at one time possessing a complex of seven halls, 75 subsidiary shrines, and more than 50 temple lodgings. === Rituals === The “Nou Nunakawa Festival,” based on the long-told myths of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto and Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, features a bridal procession, ancient threshing rites, rice-polishing rituals, and mochi pounding. The Spring Grand Festival is held every year on April 24 and is locally known as the “Nou Festival.” Eleven pieces of bugaku and a lion dance are performed, and the final dance, “Ryōō no Mai,” is especially striking, with crimson costumes set against the evening sun of early spring. The bugaku was selected in {{ill|lt=1973|Q2477|WD=Q2477}} (Shōwa 48) as a National {{ill|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property Requiring Documentation|Q11642584|WD=Q11642584}} (Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property), and in 1980 (Shōwa 55) was designated as a National {{ill|lt=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Q6573893|WD=Q6573893}} under the name “Nou Bugaku” (see also “{{ill|lt=Itoigawa–Nou Bugaku|Q11604877|WD=Q11604877}}” and “{{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku|Q11579603|WD=Q11579603}}”). === Shrine buildings === The main hall is a {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=sangensha nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|lt=cypress bark roofing|Q199388|WD=Q199388}}, with a one-bay {{ill|lt=worship porch|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970}} at the front. Built in the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} ({{ill|lt=1515|Q6208|WD=Q6208}}), it retains characteristics of the Muromachi period. In 1958 (Shōwa 33), it was designated a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. The treasure hall houses Important Cultural Properties such as a wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, as well as ship ema and ship plaques designated as {{ill|lt=Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11644858|WD=Q11644858}}. === Shrine grounds === The shrine grove, Oyama (the sacred mountain), is said to mark the northernmost habitat of the {{ill|lt=Himeharu cicada|Q5976326|WD=Q5976326}}, and in {{ill|lt=1996|Q2070|WD=Q2070}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 8) it was selected as one of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan’s 100 Soundscapes|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === (The designation type and date are shown in parentheses after each item.) ;Important Cultural Properties (National) * Main hall, with 4 ridge tags attached (structure, May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33)) * Wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, 1 figure (sculpture, April 14, {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39)) : A single-block carving of cherry wood, made in the late Heian period. Around the topknot on the head are traces indicating that faces like those seen on Eleven-Headed Kannon statues were once attached. At present, the arms from the shoulders onward are missing, and traces remain showing that multiple arms were once joined. However, the joints are too small for a Thousand-Armed Kannon, and it is presumed that the statue was originally created as an Eleven-Headed Kannon with four arms (two on each side). Eleven-Headed Kannon statues are usually two-armed, but among those created as the honji-butsu of Hakusan, there are examples with four arms.Special Exhibition Catalog, “Niigata Buddhist Sculpture Exhibition: Special Exhibition Commemorating the Recovery from the Chuetsu Earthquake,” Niigata Prefectural Museum of Modern Art, 2006, p.130 ;Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Maritime faith materials of Nou Hakusan Shrine, 97 items (March 3, 1987 (Shōwa 62)) : Includes ship ema and ship plaques. The ships depicted on the ema are called Hagase-bune. ;Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Nou Bugaku (January 28, 1980 (Shōwa 55)) ;Natural Monuments (National) * Shrine grove of Nou Hakusan Shrine (December 21, 1937 (Shōwa 12)) * Habitat of the Nou Himeharu cicada (October 14, 1942 (Shōwa 17)) ;Niigata Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Bugaku masks, 4 pieces, with 1 additional piece (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze standing Eleven-Headed Kannon statue, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Wooden seated statue of Taichō Daishi, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze temple bell, 1 piece (craft, March 31, 1976 (Shōwa 51)) ;Itoigawa City Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Worship hall (structure, July 22, 1994 (Heisei 6)) * Group of Buddhist and Shinto statues, 43 figures (sculpture, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Ancient mirrors, 2 pieces (craft, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Complete Buddhist sutra written in gold on indigo paper, 1 scroll (calligraphy, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Vermilion-sealed documents of Tokugawa Iemitsu, 11 items (calligraphy, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) * Ridge tag, 1 piece (historical material, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) === Visitor information === ;Access * About a 20-minute walk northeast from {{ill|lt=Nou Station|Q5359845|WD=Q5359845}} ;Nearby * Benten Rock (a subsidiary shrine of Hakusan Shrine: Itsukushima Shrine) * Nou Beach === See also === * {{ill|lt=Hakusan faith|Q11579557|WD=Q11579557}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsu Shrine (Itoigawa City)|Q172253|WD=Q172253}} === References === {{Reflist}} === External links === * {{Official website}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nou Hakusan Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata '新潟県糸魚川市大字能生7239', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:能生白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11611996}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Nou Hakusan Shrine |Image = [[File:Hakusan-jinja Nou town Niigata Japan.jpg|270px]]
Shrine grounds |Location = {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705|1=Niigata Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208|1=Itoigawa}} Ōaza Nou 7239 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|6|24.77|N|137|59|39.00|E|display=ti|region:JP-15|name=Nou Hakusan Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto
Izanagi-no-Mikoto
Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (candidate shrine) / Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = Traditionally: 11th year of Emperor Sujin |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, thatched with cypress bark |Other names = Hakusan Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 24|Q2529|WD=Q2529|1=April 24}} |Rituals = Bugaku (court dance), lion dance (April 24) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Niigata|label=Nou
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nou Hakusan Shrine''' (のうはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōaza Nou, {{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208|1=Itoigawa}}, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705|1=Niigata Prefecture}}. It is one of the candidate shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} “Nunakawa Shrine.” Its official name is '''Hakusan Shrine'''According to the list of shrines on the Niigata Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines website.. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Nunakawahime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto|1=Nunakawahime}}, {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}, and {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}. Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto is a goddess who has long been worshipped throughout the region centered on Itoigawa City. She is known from the 『{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}』 for her courtship and marriage with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, as well as the story of moving to Shinano together for the governance of the land. In ancient times, the {{ill|lt=Nishikubiki District|Q11629983|WD=Q11629983|1=Nishikubiki District, Niigata}} area of Niigata Prefecture was known as Nunakawa-no-shō. There are many legends and rituals related to Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto in the area surrounding Hakusan Shrine. In Ōaza Shimamichi within the city, slightly up the mountainside from the Shimamichi River (a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Nou River|Q134567545|WD=Q134567545|1=Nou River}}, formerly said to be called Nunokawa), which flows from Mount Ōsawa, there is a large rock cave said to be the birthplace of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. Downstream in Omi is Omi Hakusan Shrine, whose enshrined deities are Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{ill|lt=Kukurihime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kikurihime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}}. According to the origin legend of Omi Hakusan Shrine, the cave is also said to have been the residence when Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto married into the household of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto of Koshi. Mount Ōsawa is connected to Shindōzan (Shindō-no-mine), which is passed down in legend as the path Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto traveled to court Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. There are 1,088 stone steps leading to the summit. === History === According to a prewar “Prospectus of the Repair Donation Association,” the shrine is said to have been founded on the first Horse Day of the 11th month in the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Genshō|Q235343|WD=Q235343|1=Empress Genshō}}, the monk {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō Daishi|1=Taichō}} deeply revered the shrine, installed Buddhist statues, and named it “Hakusan Gongen.” In the {{ill|lt=Kankō|Q2736722|WD=Q2736722|1=Kankō}} era (early 11th century), a divided spirit was transferred from {{ill|lt=Shirayama Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|lt=Kaga Hakusan Shrine|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} and enshrined together at the former shrine site. Nouzan Taiheiji Hōkōin flourished as a subsidiary hall of Hakusan Gongen, at one time possessing a complex of seven halls, 75 subsidiary shrines, and more than 50 temple lodgings. === Rituals === The “Nou Nunakawa Festival,” based on the long-told myths of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto and Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, features a bridal procession, ancient threshing rites, rice-polishing rituals, and mochi pounding. The Spring Grand Festival is held every year on April 24 and is locally known as the “Nou Festival.” Eleven pieces of bugaku and a lion dance are performed, and the final dance, “Ryōō no Mai,” is especially striking, with crimson costumes set against the evening sun of early spring. The bugaku was selected in {{ill|lt=1973|Q2477|WD=Q2477|1=1973}} (Shōwa 48) as a National {{ill|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property Requiring Documentation|Q11642584|WD=Q11642584|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation}} (Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property), and in 1980 (Shōwa 55) was designated as a National {{ill|lt=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Q6573893|WD=Q6573893|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} under the name “Nou Bugaku” (see also “{{ill|lt=Itoigawa–Nou Bugaku|Q11604877|WD=Q11604877|1=Itoigawa-No's Bugaku}}” and “{{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku|Q11579603|WD=Q11579603|1=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku}}”). === Shrine buildings === The main hall is a {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=sangensha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|lt=cypress bark roofing|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}}, with a one-bay {{ill|lt=worship porch|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} at the front. Built in the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} ({{ill|lt=1515|Q6208|WD=Q6208|1=1515}}), it retains characteristics of the Muromachi period. In 1958 (Shōwa 33), it was designated a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. The treasure hall houses Important Cultural Properties such as a wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, as well as ship ema and ship plaques designated as {{ill|lt=Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11644858|WD=Q11644858|1=National Important Tangible Folk Cultural Property}}. === Shrine grounds === The shrine grove, Oyama (the sacred mountain), is said to mark the northernmost habitat of the {{ill|lt=Himeharu cicada|Q5976326|WD=Q5976326|1=Euterpnosia chibensis}}, and in {{ill|lt=1996|Q2070|WD=Q2070|1=1996}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 8) it was selected as one of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan’s 100 Soundscapes|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === (The designation type and date are shown in parentheses after each item.) ;Important Cultural Properties (National) * Main hall, with 4 ridge tags attached (structure, May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33)) * Wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, 1 figure (sculpture, April 14, {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39)) : A single-block carving of cherry wood, made in the late Heian period. Around the topknot on the head are traces indicating that faces like those seen on Eleven-Headed Kannon statues were once attached. At present, the arms from the shoulders onward are missing, and traces remain showing that multiple arms were once joined. However, the joints are too small for a Thousand-Armed Kannon, and it is presumed that the statue was originally created as an Eleven-Headed Kannon with four arms (two on each side). Eleven-Headed Kannon statues are usually two-armed, but among those created as the honji-butsu of Hakusan, there are examples with four arms.Special Exhibition Catalog, “Niigata Buddhist Sculpture Exhibition: Special Exhibition Commemorating the Recovery from the Chuetsu Earthquake,” Niigata Prefectural Museum of Modern Art, 2006, p.130 ;Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Maritime faith materials of Nou Hakusan Shrine, 97 items (March 3, 1987 (Shōwa 62)) : Includes ship ema and ship plaques. The ships depicted on the ema are called Hagase-bune. ;Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Nou Bugaku (January 28, 1980 (Shōwa 55)) ;Natural Monuments (National) * Shrine grove of Nou Hakusan Shrine (December 21, 1937 (Shōwa 12)) * Habitat of the Nou Himeharu cicada (October 14, 1942 (Shōwa 17)) ;Niigata Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Bugaku masks, 4 pieces, with 1 additional piece (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze standing Eleven-Headed Kannon statue, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Wooden seated statue of Taichō Daishi, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze temple bell, 1 piece (craft, March 31, 1976 (Shōwa 51)) ;Itoigawa City Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Worship hall (structure, July 22, 1994 (Heisei 6)) * Group of Buddhist and Shinto statues, 43 figures (sculpture, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Ancient mirrors, 2 pieces (craft, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Complete Buddhist sutra written in gold on indigo paper, 1 scroll (calligraphy, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Vermilion-sealed documents of Tokugawa Iemitsu, 11 items (calligraphy, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) * Ridge tag, 1 piece (historical material, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) === Visitor information === ;Access * About a 20-minute walk northeast from {{ill|lt=Nou Station|Q5359845|WD=Q5359845|1=Nō Station}} ;Nearby * Benten Rock (a subsidiary shrine of Hakusan Shrine: Itsukushima Shrine) * Nou Beach === See also === * {{ill|lt=Hakusan faith|Q11579557|WD=Q11579557|1=Hakusan Faith}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsu Shrine (Itoigawa City)|Q172253|WD=Q172253|1=Amatsu Shrine}} === References === {{Reflist}} === External links === * {{Official website}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nou Hakusan Shrine}} == {{ill|Nunakahano Shrine|lt=Nunakahano Shrine|WD=Q135040414|1=Nunakahano Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '新潟県糸魚川市大字能生7239', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:能生白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11611996}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Nou Hakusan Shrine |Image = [[File:Hakusan-jinja Nou town Niigata Japan.jpg|270px]]
Shrine grounds |Location = {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705|1=Niigata Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208|1=Itoigawa}} Ōaza Nou 7239 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|6|24.77|N|137|59|39.00|E|display=ti|region:JP-15|name=Nou Hakusan Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto
Izanagi-no-Mikoto
Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (candidate shrine) / Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = Traditionally: 11th year of Emperor Sujin |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, thatched with cypress bark |Other names = Hakusan Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 24|Q2529|WD=Q2529|1=April 24}} |Rituals = Bugaku (court dance), lion dance (April 24) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Niigata|label=Nou
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nou Hakusan Shrine''' (のうはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōaza Nou, {{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208|1=Itoigawa}}, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705|1=Niigata Prefecture}}. It is one of the candidate shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} “Nunakawa Shrine.” Its official name is '''Hakusan Shrine'''According to the list of shrines on the Niigata Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines website.. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Nunakawahime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto|1=Nunakawahime}}, {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}, and {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}. Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto is a goddess who has long been worshipped throughout the region centered on Itoigawa City. She is known from the 『{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}』 for her courtship and marriage with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, as well as the story of moving to Shinano together for the governance of the land. In ancient times, the {{ill|lt=Nishikubiki District|Q11629983|WD=Q11629983|1=Nishikubiki District, Niigata}} area of Niigata Prefecture was known as Nunakawa-no-shō. There are many legends and rituals related to Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto in the area surrounding Hakusan Shrine. In Ōaza Shimamichi within the city, slightly up the mountainside from the Shimamichi River (a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Nou River|Q134567545|WD=Q134567545|1=Nou River}}, formerly said to be called Nunokawa), which flows from Mount Ōsawa, there is a large rock cave said to be the birthplace of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. Downstream in Omi is Omi Hakusan Shrine, whose enshrined deities are Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{ill|lt=Kukurihime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kikurihime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}}. According to the origin legend of Omi Hakusan Shrine, the cave is also said to have been the residence when Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto married into the household of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto of Koshi. Mount Ōsawa is connected to Shindōzan (Shindō-no-mine), which is passed down in legend as the path Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto traveled to court Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. There are 1,088 stone steps leading to the summit. === History === According to a prewar “Prospectus of the Repair Donation Association,” the shrine is said to have been founded on the first Horse Day of the 11th month in the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Genshō|Q235343|WD=Q235343|1=Empress Genshō}}, the monk {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō Daishi|1=Taichō}} deeply revered the shrine, installed Buddhist statues, and named it “Hakusan Gongen.” In the {{ill|lt=Kankō|Q2736722|WD=Q2736722|1=Kankō}} era (early 11th century), a divided spirit was transferred from {{ill|lt=Shirayama Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|lt=Kaga Hakusan Shrine|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} and enshrined together at the former shrine site. Nouzan Taiheiji Hōkōin flourished as a subsidiary hall of Hakusan Gongen, at one time possessing a complex of seven halls, 75 subsidiary shrines, and more than 50 temple lodgings. === Rituals === The “Nou Nunakawa Festival,” based on the long-told myths of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto and Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, features a bridal procession, ancient threshing rites, rice-polishing rituals, and mochi pounding. The Spring Grand Festival is held every year on April 24 and is locally known as the “Nou Festival.” Eleven pieces of bugaku and a lion dance are performed, and the final dance, “Ryōō no Mai,” is especially striking, with crimson costumes set against the evening sun of early spring. The bugaku was selected in {{ill|lt=1973|Q2477|WD=Q2477|1=1973}} (Shōwa 48) as a National {{ill|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property Requiring Documentation|Q11642584|WD=Q11642584|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation}} (Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property), and in 1980 (Shōwa 55) was designated as a National {{ill|lt=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Q6573893|WD=Q6573893|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} under the name “Nou Bugaku” (see also “{{ill|lt=Itoigawa–Nou Bugaku|Q11604877|WD=Q11604877|1=Itoigawa-No's Bugaku}}” and “{{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku|Q11579603|WD=Q11579603|1=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku}}”). === Shrine buildings === The main hall is a {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=sangensha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|lt=cypress bark roofing|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}}, with a one-bay {{ill|lt=worship porch|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} at the front. Built in the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} ({{ill|lt=1515|Q6208|WD=Q6208|1=1515}}), it retains characteristics of the Muromachi period. In 1958 (Shōwa 33), it was designated a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. The treasure hall houses Important Cultural Properties such as a wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, as well as ship ema and ship plaques designated as {{ill|lt=Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11644858|WD=Q11644858|1=National Important Tangible Folk Cultural Property}}. === Shrine grounds === The shrine grove, Oyama (the sacred mountain), is said to mark the northernmost habitat of the {{ill|lt=Himeharu cicada|Q5976326|WD=Q5976326|1=Euterpnosia chibensis}}, and in {{ill|lt=1996|Q2070|WD=Q2070|1=1996}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 8) it was selected as one of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan’s 100 Soundscapes|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === (The designation type and date are shown in parentheses after each item.) ;Important Cultural Properties (National) * Main hall, with 4 ridge tags attached (structure, May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33)) * Wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, 1 figure (sculpture, April 14, {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39)) : A single-block carving of cherry wood, made in the late Heian period. Around the topknot on the head are traces indicating that faces like those seen on Eleven-Headed Kannon statues were once attached. At present, the arms from the shoulders onward are missing, and traces remain showing that multiple arms were once joined. However, the joints are too small for a Thousand-Armed Kannon, and it is presumed that the statue was originally created as an Eleven-Headed Kannon with four arms (two on each side). Eleven-Headed Kannon statues are usually two-armed, but among those created as the honji-butsu of Hakusan, there are examples with four arms.Special Exhibition Catalog, “Niigata Buddhist Sculpture Exhibition: Special Exhibition Commemorating the Recovery from the Chuetsu Earthquake,” Niigata Prefectural Museum of Modern Art, 2006, p.130 ;Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Maritime faith materials of Nou Hakusan Shrine, 97 items (March 3, 1987 (Shōwa 62)) : Includes ship ema and ship plaques. The ships depicted on the ema are called Hagase-bune. ;Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Nou Bugaku (January 28, 1980 (Shōwa 55)) ;Natural Monuments (National) * Shrine grove of Nou Hakusan Shrine (December 21, 1937 (Shōwa 12)) * Habitat of the Nou Himeharu cicada (October 14, 1942 (Shōwa 17)) ;Niigata Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Bugaku masks, 4 pieces, with 1 additional piece (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze standing Eleven-Headed Kannon statue, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Wooden seated statue of Taichō Daishi, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze temple bell, 1 piece (craft, March 31, 1976 (Shōwa 51)) ;Itoigawa City Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Worship hall (structure, July 22, 1994 (Heisei 6)) * Group of Buddhist and Shinto statues, 43 figures (sculpture, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Ancient mirrors, 2 pieces (craft, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Complete Buddhist sutra written in gold on indigo paper, 1 scroll (calligraphy, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Vermilion-sealed documents of Tokugawa Iemitsu, 11 items (calligraphy, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) * Ridge tag, 1 piece (historical material, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) === Visitor information === ;Access * About a 20-minute walk northeast from {{ill|lt=Nou Station|Q5359845|WD=Q5359845|1=Nō Station}} ;Nearby * Benten Rock (a subsidiary shrine of Hakusan Shrine: Itsukushima Shrine) * Nou Beach === See also === * {{ill|lt=Hakusan faith|Q11579557|WD=Q11579557|1=Hakusan Faith}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsu Shrine (Itoigawa City)|Q172253|WD=Q172253|1=Amatsu Shrine}} === References === {{Reflist}} === External links === * {{Official website}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nou Hakusan Shrine}} == {{ill|Nunakahano Shrine|lt=Nunakahano Shrine|WD=Q135040414|1=Nunakahano Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '新潟県糸魚川市大字能生7239', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:能生白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11611996}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Nou Hakusan Shrine |Image = [[File:Hakusan-jinja Nou town Niigata Japan.jpg|270px]]
Shrine grounds |Location = {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705|1=Niigata Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208|1=Itoigawa}} Ōaza Nou 7239 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|6|24.77|N|137|59|39.00|E|display=ti|region:JP-15|name=Nou Hakusan Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto
Izanagi-no-Mikoto
Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (candidate shrine) / Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = Traditionally: 11th year of Emperor Sujin |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, thatched with cypress bark |Other names = Hakusan Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 24|Q2529|WD=Q2529|1=April 24}} |Rituals = Bugaku (court dance), lion dance (April 24) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Niigata|label=Nou
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nou Hakusan Shrine''' (のうはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōaza Nou, {{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208|1=Itoigawa}}, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705|1=Niigata Prefecture}}. It is one of the candidate shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} “Nunakawa Shrine.” Its official name is '''Hakusan Shrine'''According to the list of shrines on the Niigata Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines website.. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Nunakawahime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto|1=Nunakawahime}}, {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}, and {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}. Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto is a goddess who has long been worshipped throughout the region centered on Itoigawa City. She is known from the 『{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}』 for her courtship and marriage with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, as well as the story of moving to Shinano together for the governance of the land. In ancient times, the {{ill|lt=Nishikubiki District|Q11629983|WD=Q11629983|1=Nishikubiki District, Niigata}} area of Niigata Prefecture was known as Nunakawa-no-shō. There are many legends and rituals related to Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto in the area surrounding Hakusan Shrine. In Ōaza Shimamichi within the city, slightly up the mountainside from the Shimamichi River (a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Nou River|Q134567545|WD=Q134567545|1=Nou River}}, formerly said to be called Nunokawa), which flows from Mount Ōsawa, there is a large rock cave said to be the birthplace of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. Downstream in Omi is Omi Hakusan Shrine, whose enshrined deities are Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{ill|lt=Kukurihime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kikurihime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}}. According to the origin legend of Omi Hakusan Shrine, the cave is also said to have been the residence when Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto married into the household of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto of Koshi. Mount Ōsawa is connected to Shindōzan (Shindō-no-mine), which is passed down in legend as the path Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto traveled to court Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. There are 1,088 stone steps leading to the summit. === History === According to a prewar “Prospectus of the Repair Donation Association,” the shrine is said to have been founded on the first Horse Day of the 11th month in the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Genshō|Q235343|WD=Q235343|1=Empress Genshō}}, the monk {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō Daishi|1=Taichō}} deeply revered the shrine, installed Buddhist statues, and named it “Hakusan Gongen.” In the {{ill|lt=Kankō|Q2736722|WD=Q2736722|1=Kankō}} era (early 11th century), a divided spirit was transferred from {{ill|lt=Shirayama Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|lt=Kaga Hakusan Shrine|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} and enshrined together at the former shrine site. Nouzan Taiheiji Hōkōin flourished as a subsidiary hall of Hakusan Gongen, at one time possessing a complex of seven halls, 75 subsidiary shrines, and more than 50 temple lodgings. === Rituals === The “Nou Nunakawa Festival,” based on the long-told myths of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto and Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, features a bridal procession, ancient threshing rites, rice-polishing rituals, and mochi pounding. The Spring Grand Festival is held every year on April 24 and is locally known as the “Nou Festival.” Eleven pieces of bugaku and a lion dance are performed, and the final dance, “Ryōō no Mai,” is especially striking, with crimson costumes set against the evening sun of early spring. The bugaku was selected in {{ill|lt=1973|Q2477|WD=Q2477|1=1973}} (Shōwa 48) as a National {{ill|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property Requiring Documentation|Q11642584|WD=Q11642584|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation}} (Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property), and in 1980 (Shōwa 55) was designated as a National {{ill|lt=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Q6573893|WD=Q6573893|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} under the name “Nou Bugaku” (see also “{{ill|lt=Itoigawa–Nou Bugaku|Q11604877|WD=Q11604877|1=Itoigawa-No's Bugaku}}” and “{{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku|Q11579603|WD=Q11579603|1=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku}}”). === Shrine buildings === The main hall is a {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=sangensha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|lt=cypress bark roofing|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}}, with a one-bay {{ill|lt=worship porch|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} at the front. Built in the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} ({{ill|lt=1515|Q6208|WD=Q6208|1=1515}}), it retains characteristics of the Muromachi period. In 1958 (Shōwa 33), it was designated a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. The treasure hall houses Important Cultural Properties such as a wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, as well as ship ema and ship plaques designated as {{ill|lt=Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11644858|WD=Q11644858|1=National Important Tangible Folk Cultural Property}}. === Shrine grounds === The shrine grove, Oyama (the sacred mountain), is said to mark the northernmost habitat of the {{ill|lt=Himeharu cicada|Q5976326|WD=Q5976326|1=Euterpnosia chibensis}}, and in {{ill|lt=1996|Q2070|WD=Q2070|1=1996}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 8) it was selected as one of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan’s 100 Soundscapes|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === (The designation type and date are shown in parentheses after each item.) ;Important Cultural Properties (National) * Main hall, with 4 ridge tags attached (structure, May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33)) * Wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, 1 figure (sculpture, April 14, {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39)) : A single-block carving of cherry wood, made in the late Heian period. Around the topknot on the head are traces indicating that faces like those seen on Eleven-Headed Kannon statues were once attached. At present, the arms from the shoulders onward are missing, and traces remain showing that multiple arms were once joined. However, the joints are too small for a Thousand-Armed Kannon, and it is presumed that the statue was originally created as an Eleven-Headed Kannon with four arms (two on each side). Eleven-Headed Kannon statues are usually two-armed, but among those created as the honji-butsu of Hakusan, there are examples with four arms.Special Exhibition Catalog, “Niigata Buddhist Sculpture Exhibition: Special Exhibition Commemorating the Recovery from the Chuetsu Earthquake,” Niigata Prefectural Museum of Modern Art, 2006, p.130 ;Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Maritime faith materials of Nou Hakusan Shrine, 97 items (March 3, 1987 (Shōwa 62)) : Includes ship ema and ship plaques. The ships depicted on the ema are called Hagase-bune. ;Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Nou Bugaku (January 28, 1980 (Shōwa 55)) ;Natural Monuments (National) * Shrine grove of Nou Hakusan Shrine (December 21, 1937 (Shōwa 12)) * Habitat of the Nou Himeharu cicada (October 14, 1942 (Shōwa 17)) ;Niigata Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Bugaku masks, 4 pieces, with 1 additional piece (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze standing Eleven-Headed Kannon statue, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Wooden seated statue of Taichō Daishi, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze temple bell, 1 piece (craft, March 31, 1976 (Shōwa 51)) ;Itoigawa City Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Worship hall (structure, July 22, 1994 (Heisei 6)) * Group of Buddhist and Shinto statues, 43 figures (sculpture, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Ancient mirrors, 2 pieces (craft, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Complete Buddhist sutra written in gold on indigo paper, 1 scroll (calligraphy, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Vermilion-sealed documents of Tokugawa Iemitsu, 11 items (calligraphy, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) * Ridge tag, 1 piece (historical material, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) === Visitor information === ;Access * About a 20-minute walk northeast from {{ill|lt=Nou Station|Q5359845|WD=Q5359845|1=Nō Station}} ;Nearby * Benten Rock (a subsidiary shrine of Hakusan Shrine: Itsukushima Shrine) * Nou Beach === See also === * {{ill|lt=Hakusan faith|Q11579557|WD=Q11579557|1=Hakusan Faith}} * {{ill|lt=Amatsu Shrine (Itoigawa City)|Q172253|WD=Q172253|1=Amatsu Shrine}} === References === {{Reflist}} === External links === * {{Official website}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated {{ill|Nunakahano Shrine|lt=Nunakahano Shrine|WD=Q135040414|1=Nunakahano Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '新潟県糸魚川市大字能生7239', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:能生白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11611996}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Nou Hakusan Shrine |Image = [[File:Hakusan-jinja Nou town Niigata Japan.jpg|270px]]
Shrine grounds |Location = {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705|1=Niigata Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208|1=Itoigawa}} Ōaza Nou 7239 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|6|24.77|N|137|59|39.00|E|display=ti|region:JP-15|name=Nou Hakusan Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto
Izanagi-no-Mikoto
Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (candidate shrine) / Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = Traditionally: 11th year of Emperor Sujin |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, thatched with cypress bark |Other names = Hakusan Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 24|Q2529|WD=Q2529|1=April 24}} |Rituals = Bugaku (court dance), lion dance (April 24) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Niigata|label=Nou
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nou Hakusan Shrine''' (のうはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōaza Nou, {{ill|lt=Itoigawa City|Q857208|WD=Q857208|1=Itoigawa}}, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705|1=Niigata Prefecture}}. It is one of the candidate shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} “Nunakawa Shrine.” Its official name is '''Hakusan Shrine'''According to the list of shrines on the Niigata Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines website.. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Nunakawahime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto|1=Nunakawahime}}, {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}, and {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}. Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto is a goddess who has long been worshipped throughout the region centered on Itoigawa City. She is known from the 『{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}』 for her courtship and marriage with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, as well as the story of moving to Shinano together for the governance of the land. In ancient times, the {{ill|lt=Nishikubiki District|Q11629983|WD=Q11629983|1=Nishikubiki District, Niigata}} area of Niigata Prefecture was known as Nunakawa-no-shō. There are many legends and rituals related to Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto in the area surrounding Hakusan Shrine. In Ōaza Shimamichi within the city, slightly up the mountainside from the Shimamichi River (a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Nou River|Q134567545|WD=Q134567545|1=Nou River}}, formerly said to be called Nunokawa), which flows from Mount Ōsawa, there is a large rock cave said to be the birthplace of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. Downstream in Omi is Omi Hakusan Shrine, whose enshrined deities are Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{ill|lt=Kukurihime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kikurihime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}}. According to the origin legend of Omi Hakusan Shrine, the cave is also said to have been the residence when Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto married into the household of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto of Koshi. Mount Ōsawa is connected to Shindōzan (Shindō-no-mine), which is passed down in legend as the path Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto traveled to court Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto. There are 1,088 stone steps leading to the summit. === History === According to a prewar “Prospectus of the Repair Donation Association,” the shrine is said to have been founded on the first Horse Day of the 11th month in the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Genshō|Q235343|WD=Q235343|1=Empress Genshō}}, the monk {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō Daishi|1=Taichō}} deeply revered the shrine, installed Buddhist statues, and named it “Hakusan Gongen.” In the {{ill|lt=Kankō|Q2736722|WD=Q2736722|1=Kankō}} era (early 11th century), a divided spirit was transferred from {{ill|lt=Shirayama Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|lt=Kaga Hakusan Shrine|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} and enshrined together at the former shrine site. Nouzan Taiheiji Hōkōin flourished as a subsidiary hall of Hakusan Gongen, at one time possessing a complex of seven halls, 75 subsidiary shrines, and more than 50 temple lodgings. === Rituals === The “Nou Nunakawa Festival,” based on the long-told myths of Nunakawahime-no-Mikoto and Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, features a bridal procession, ancient threshing rites, rice-polishing rituals, and mochi pounding. The Spring Grand Festival is held every year on April 24 and is locally known as the “Nou Festival.” Eleven pieces of bugaku and a lion dance are performed, and the final dance, “Ryōō no Mai,” is especially striking, with crimson costumes set against the evening sun of early spring. The bugaku was selected in {{ill|lt=1973|Q2477|WD=Q2477|1=1973}} (Shōwa 48) as a National {{ill|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property Requiring Documentation|Q11642584|WD=Q11642584|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation}} (Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property), and in 1980 (Shōwa 55) was designated as a National {{ill|lt=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Q6573893|WD=Q6573893|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} under the name “Nou Bugaku” (see also “{{ill|lt=Itoigawa–Nou Bugaku|Q11604877|WD=Q11604877|1=Itoigawa-No's Bugaku}}” and “{{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku|Q11579603|WD=Q11579603|1=Hakusan Shrine Bugaku}}”). === Shrine buildings === The main hall is a {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=sangensha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|lt=cypress bark roofing|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}}, with a one-bay {{ill|lt=worship porch|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} at the front. Built in the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} ({{ill|lt=1515|Q6208|WD=Q6208|1=1515}}), it retains characteristics of the Muromachi period. In 1958 (Shōwa 33), it was designated a National {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. The treasure hall houses Important Cultural Properties such as a wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, as well as ship ema and ship plaques designated as {{ill|lt=Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties|Q11644858|WD=Q11644858|1=National Important Tangible Folk Cultural Property}}. === Shrine grounds === The shrine grove, Oyama (the sacred mountain), is said to mark the northernmost habitat of the {{ill|lt=Himeharu cicada|Q5976326|WD=Q5976326|1=Euterpnosia chibensis}}, and in {{ill|lt=1996|Q2070|WD=Q2070|1=1996}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 8) it was selected as one of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japan’s 100 Soundscapes|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === (The designation type and date are shown in parentheses after each item.) ;Important Cultural Properties (National) * Main hall, with 4 ridge tags attached (structure, May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33)) * Wooden standing statue of Shō Kannon, 1 figure (sculpture, April 14, {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39)) : A single-block carving of cherry wood, made in the late Heian period. Around the topknot on the head are traces indicating that faces like those seen on Eleven-Headed Kannon statues were once attached. At present, the arms from the shoulders onward are missing, and traces remain showing that multiple arms were once joined. However, the joints are too small for a Thousand-Armed Kannon, and it is presumed that the statue was originally created as an Eleven-Headed Kannon with four arms (two on each side). Eleven-Headed Kannon statues are usually two-armed, but among those created as the honji-butsu of Hakusan, there are examples with four arms.Special Exhibition Catalog, “Niigata Buddhist Sculpture Exhibition: Special Exhibition Commemorating the Recovery from the Chuetsu Earthquake,” Niigata Prefectural Museum of Modern Art, 2006, p.130 ;Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Maritime faith materials of Nou Hakusan Shrine, 97 items (March 3, 1987 (Shōwa 62)) : Includes ship ema and ship plaques. The ships depicted on the ema are called Hagase-bune. ;Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties (National) * Nou Bugaku (January 28, 1980 (Shōwa 55)) ;Natural Monuments (National) * Shrine grove of Nou Hakusan Shrine (December 21, 1937 (Shōwa 12)) * Habitat of the Nou Himeharu cicada (October 14, 1942 (Shōwa 17)) ;Niigata Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Bugaku masks, 4 pieces, with 1 additional piece (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze standing Eleven-Headed Kannon statue, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Wooden seated statue of Taichō Daishi, 1 figure (sculpture, March 27, 1981 (Shōwa 56)) * Bronze temple bell, 1 piece (craft, March 31, 1976 (Shōwa 51)) ;Itoigawa City Designated Tangible Cultural Properties * Worship hall (structure, July 22, 1994 (Heisei 6)) * Group of Buddhist and Shinto statues, 43 figures (sculpture, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Ancient mirrors, 2 pieces (craft, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Complete Buddhist sutra written in gold on indigo paper, 1 scroll (calligraphy, May 12, 1972 (Shōwa 47)) * Vermilion-sealed documents of Tokugawa Iemitsu, 11 items (calligraphy, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) * Ridge tag, 1 piece (historical material, May 29, 1984 (Shōwa 59)) === Visitor information === ;Access * About a 20-minute walk northeast from {{ill|lt=Nou Station|Q5359845|WD=Q5359845|1=Nō Station}}
